voice: naturalist, third-person (the animal is observed, not the speaker)
rules: Keep the 8 locked questions as the structural spine of any answer set.
Answers may use the full master bullet list and scientific vocabulary.
Be explicit when something is broadly accepted vs. still debated.
You may extend beyond the 8 questions only when a user asks a follow-up.
Cite scientific name, habitat, and conservation status when discussing the species.
Never pretend to be the animal — third-person, naturalist voice only.
locked_questions: What can it see, hear, or feel?
Where does it live — and why?
What does it do when something changes?

What can it see, hear, or feel?: Its flickering tongue picks up chemical particles from the air and surfaces.
Its eyes detect movement in dim forest light at night.
Vibrations through its feet and tail tell it about activity on nearby branches.
It smells the scent of group members to recognize its own family group.
Where does it live — and why?: It lives in the tree canopy of tropical forests on the Solomon Islands.
It eats mainly the leaves, fruits, and growing tips of specific forest plants.
Its prehensile tail grips branches so it can hang and reach food.
It rarely comes to the ground, spending nearly all its time in trees.
How does it send messages?: It hisses sharply as a warning when it feels threatened.
It will bite hard if its hiss warning is ignored.
Group members rub scent on shared branches to mark their territory.
Slow head movements signal calm recognition of a known group member.
What does it do when something changes?: When threatened, it grips tighter with its tail and presses close to the branch.
It turns sideways to look larger when facing a perceived threat.
Strange animals approaching the group territory are met with aggressive hissing.
It moves deeper into dense foliage when disturbed during the day.
How does it learn?: Young skinks stay inside the family group for six to twelve months after birth.
Adults and unrelated group members protect the young as they learn the canopy.
Juveniles observe which plants adults eat before foraging on their own.
How does it have babies?: The female carries young inside her body for six to eight months.
She gives birth to one large, live offspring — unusual for a reptile.
Both parents and other group members guard the newborn skink.
The newborn is already able to grip branches and move through the canopy.
What are its limits?: It cannot survive without the specific forest plants it eats on the Solomon Islands.
Its group needs a defended territory of trees to feed and shelter within.
Collection for the pet trade has sharply reduced wild populations.
Deforestation removes both food plants and the branches it needs to move through.
What does this teach us?: A reptile that lives in family groups and protects young is rare.
Every behavior — tongue flick, hiss, tail grip — carries specific information.
The canopy forest must stay intact for the whole group to survive.

closing_prompt: LOOK AT THE ANIMAL: What do you see it doing right now?
footer: What a living thing can sense becomes its reality.

alpha_omega_failure_cycle.description: Four-part per-species failure cycle. The Tier 2 LLM walks through these lists before answering a hard question about this species: what is solid, what is contested, how people commonly get it wrong, and what this species' hard rules are. Content is species-specific and derived from master bullets.
broadly_accepted: One of the only reptiles known to live in stable family groups.
Gives birth to one unusually large, live young — rare among lizards.
Fully arboreal, using a prehensile tail as a fifth limb in island forest.
Diet is specialized on specific forest plant species in the Solomon Islands.
still_debated: How group members coordinate defense of shared branches.
Whether tongue-flicking patterns encode recognition of specific individuals.
The precise impact of pet-trade collection on remaining island populations.
common_failure_modes: Describing it as solitary like most reptiles — family group life is a key feature.
Assuming diet overlap with other skinks — it is highly specialized.
Confusing its prehensile tail with that of a primate or possum.
species_specific_guardrails: Do not describe it as egg-laying; it is live-bearing.
Do not claim its diet 'works for any skink' — it is species-specific.
Do not imply it is low-risk; IUCN lists it as Vulnerable.
